era_context: Mid-7th century CE: The Islamic Caliphate is in crisis following the death of Caliph Uthman. The First Fitna (Islamic civil war) is beginning. The Byzantine Empire under Constans II is recovering from the Arab-Byzantine wars. Tang Dynasty China under Emperor Gaozong is consolidating power. In Europe, Merovingian Frankish kingdoms are fragmenting; the Visigothic Kingdom in Iberia is in decline.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Assassination of Caliph Uthman and election of Ali ibn Abi Talib
events.description: Caliph Uthman ibn Affan (r. 644–656 CE) was assassinated by rebellious troops in Medina in June 656 CE. His death was the result of growing discontent over his nepotism, his distribution of conquered lands to his Umayyad relatives, and his perceived weakness in military affairs. Following Uthman's death, Ali ibn Abi Talib was elected as the fourth Caliph by the Muslim community in Medina. However, Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan, the Umayyad governor of the Levant, refused to recognize Ali's authority, claiming that Uthman's murderers should be punished first. This dispute led to the First Fitna (Islamic civil war), which would last until 661 CE and fundamentally shape Islamic history, leading to the emergence of the Umayyad Caliphate and the crystallization of the Sunni-Shia split.
key_figures: Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan (Umayyad governor, rival claimant)
Aisha bint Abi Bakr (widow of Prophet Muhammad, opponent of Ali)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled 9th century
Early Islamic historical tradition (consensus of later sources)

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Uthman and the election of Ali are well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date of June 656 CE is widely accepted, though some sources vary by a few months.

events.title: Battle of the Camel (Jamal)
events.description: The Battle of the Camel (December 656 CE) was fought between the forces of Caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib and a coalition led by Aisha bint Abi Bakr (widow of Prophet Muhammad), Talha ibn Ubaydallah, and Zubayr ibn al-Awwam. The battle took place near Basra in Iraq and resulted in Ali's victory. Aisha's forces were defeated, and she was captured and later released. The battle is named after the camel on which Aisha rode during the battle. This was one of the first major battles of the First Fitna and marked a significant victory for Ali, though it also deepened the divisions within the Muslim community.
key_figures: Aisha bint Abi Bakr (widow of Prophet Muhammad, opponent of Ali)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled 9th century
Early Islamic historical tradition (consensus of later sources)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Camel is well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date of December 656 CE is widely accepted, though some sources vary by a few months.
cross_references: 656-001 (Assassination of Uthman and election of Ali)

events.title: Tang Dynasty consolidation and military expansion
events.description: Emperor Gaozong of Tang (r. 649–683 CE) continued to consolidate power and expand Tang military dominance in Central Asia. By 656 CE, Tang forces had defeated the Western Turks and were establishing hegemony over the Silk Road trade routes. This military success underpinned Tang economic prosperity and cultural influence across Eurasia.

sources.name: Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu), compiled 945 CE
New Book of Tang (Xin Tangshu), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: Tang military campaigns in Central Asia are well-documented in Chinese historiography, though specific events in 656 CE are not always precisely dated. The broader pattern of Tang expansion is confirmed.

events.title: Byzantine military campaigns under Constans II
events.description: Emperor Constans II of the Byzantine Empire (r. 641–668 CE) continued military campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean and Anatolia. By 656 CE, Constans II was attempting to stabilize Byzantine control over key territories and maintain naval superiority against Arab expansion. These campaigns were part of the broader Arab-Byzantine wars that had been ongoing since the 630s CE.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle (Chronographia), compiled early 9th century

events.certainty_note: Byzantine military campaigns are well-attested, but specific engagements in 656 CE are not precisely documented. Dating is approximate within ±5 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The assassination of Uthman and the Battle of the Camel are well-attested in Islamic historiography, but modern scholars have noted that later sources (compiled centuries after the events) may have been influenced by sectarian biases. Some details of the battles and the motivations of the participants are disputed. However, the broad outline of events and their significance in Islamic history is confirmed.
historiographic_note: 656 CE is moderately well-documented for the Islamic world, with the assassination of Uthman and the Battle of the Camel being major events attested in multiple later sources. However, contemporary documentation is limited; most information comes from chronicles compiled centuries after the events. Byzantine and Chinese sources are available but less detailed for this specific year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Islamic Caliphate and Tang China; other regions are nearly absent. The First Fitna is a pivotal moment in Islamic history, and the events of 656 CE are foundational to understanding the subsequent development of Islamic civilization.

graph_edges.note: The assassination of Uthman and election of Ali led directly to the Battle of the Camel.
The Battle of the Camel was followed by the Battle of Siffin in 657 CE.
The assassination of Uthman and the subsequent First Fitna led to Muawiyah's establishment of the Umayyad Caliphate in 661 CE.
